topic: Traits - PartialOrd vs Ord and Derived Ordering

turns.content: Hey, I'm trying to fix a failing CI/CD pipeline. The build is breaking because of some ordering issues in my Rust code. Can you help me understand the difference between `PartialOrd` and `Ord`, and when deriving them?

Sure! Imagine you have two structs, say `Person` with fields like `name` and `age`. You want to compare these based on both name and age. If you derive `PartialOrd`, Rust will automatically implement it by comparing the fields one by one—first `name`, then `age`. But if any field is incomparable (like a nullable string), that comparison will return `None`. This helps avoid panics when you have partially defined data, which is common in CI/CD pipelines where some inputs might be missing or inconsistent.
So if I derive both `PartialOrd` and `Ord`, Rust just compares fields lexicographically?

Yes, exactly. If you derive both traits, the comparison is done field-by-field in order of declaration. For example, for two `Person`s: if their `name` are equal, it will compare `age`. If `name` differs, that's the result; otherwise, it goes to `age`. This can simplify your code and reduce boilerplate. Just be aware that with derived traits, you need all fields to implement both traits themselves.
What about when I want more control? Like customizing how comparisons work?

If deriving isn't enough—maybe because some of your fields don't implement `Ord` or you need special logic—you'd manually implement either `PartialOrd::partial_cmp` or `Ord::cmp`. This gives you precise control. For example, if you want to ignore case when comparing strings, or handle partial data gracefully, you'd write your own comparison function that returns a `std::cmp::Ordering`. The `Option<Ordering>` return type allows for partial ordering cases, which is important in pipelines where inputs aren't always complete.
Oh wait, I see. So if my data isn't fully defined sometimes, deriving won’t work?

Right! Deriving assumes your fields all implement `PartialOrd` or `Ord`. If you have optional fields or nullable values, deriving might panic on comparison because it can't handle `None`. That's why in CI/CD pipelines where data may be incomplete, implementing `partial_cmp` and returning `Option<Ordering>` is safer. It lets your code gracefully skip comparisons when inputs aren't fully defined.
What happens if I compare two structs that don't derive either trait? Will Rust just panic?

No, not exactly. If you try to compare two structs without deriving `PartialOrd` or `Ord`, the compiler won't allow it unless they both implement one of those traits. But if your struct fields don't implement the necessary traits either, calling comparison methods will lead to a compile-time error—Rust won’t generate code that panics unexpectedly.
So, in summary: derive when possible for simplicity; manually implement only when you need control or partial ordering?

Yes! Deriving is quick and reduces boilerplate, especially if your fields already satisfy the traits. But for complex cases—like ignoring case when comparing strings—or dealing with optional data—you'll want to write your own comparisons to have full control and avoid panics during CI/CD runs.
